summary: Fan-out design review of the airtime & mobile-delivery simulator spec

reviews.lens: MEASUREMENT-VALIDITY — will this measure the airtime ceiling correctly, and distinguish a contention/airtime-limited collapse from a connectivity/buffer/TTL effect?
strengths: The spec correctly identifies the central validity risk itself: it names the binding-constraint diagnostic (fraction of contacts where the airtime budget actually bound) and states that if it is ~0 across the sweep 'we are still measuring connectivity, not airtime (and must say so).' This is exactly the right instrument and the right honesty. It must be elevated from a diagnostic to a GATE (see findings).

The fidelity gate (reproduce analytic logistic SI growth + a multi-hop-over-time check) is the right move before trusting any dynamic-engine curve, and the spec is candid that the dynamic engine is 'comparatively unproven' relative to slice-1's validated engine-free static path. The slice-1 oracle KAT (engine fixpoint == union-find same-component pairs) is a strong precedent for this kind of independent cross-check.

Sweeping the airtime budget at >=3 levels (binding/marginal/non-binding) is the correct experimental design for isolating the airtime effect: it gives a within-experiment control where the connectivity substrate is held fixed and only the budget moves, so a curve that changes shape across budget levels is attributable to airtime rather than topology.

The 'every number is an UPPER BOUND' framing and the parameter-provenance table requirement (cited BLE figures, not invented numbers) are good measurement hygiene and consistent with the existing README's bias-direction table.

Slice-1 infrastructure being reused is genuinely sound for validity: per-replication (seed-level, not message-level) Student-t CIs via mean_ci, the fair-chance denominator in metrics.py that excludes right-censored end-of-run messages, the RWP stationarity acceptance gate (stationarity_ok), and the disjoint-substream RNG contract are all already correct and directly support clean measurement.

findings.title: Contention proxy n_local conflates connectivity degree with airtime contenders — the headline 'delivery falls with density' may be a pure artifact of the budget formula, not emergent contention
findings.issue: In engine.py _settle, n_local = max(deg[i], deg[j]) where deg is the unit-disk graph degree (count of nodes within radius r). budget.py then computes eff = throughput_ideal/(1+alpha*n_local). But graph degree IS density (d = lambda*pi*r^2). So the budget mechanically divides throughput by a quantity that scales linearly with the density axis. Any down-slope in delivery-vs-density therefore CANNOT be distinguished from the trivial fact that you hard-coded throughput to fall as 1/(1+alpha*density). This is precisely the confounder the lens exists to catch: the curve will 'collapse with density' by construction, whether or not real BLE airtime would. The validated slice-1 finding (delivery RISES monotonically with degree via S^2) is engine-free; the moment you turn on this budget, the down-turn is baked in.
findings.fix: Decouple the contention count from graph degree at the measurement level. (1) Add the binding-fraction gate (below) so a down-turn is only credited as 'airtime' if the budget actually bound on a non-trivial fraction of contacts. (2) Report the curve at alpha=0 (budget non-binding) as a control overlay on the SAME axes: if alpha=0 already turns over, the turnover is connectivity/buffer/TTL, not airtime. (3) Make the provenance table justify the mapping from 'nodes within r' to 'co-channel BLE contenders' explicitly — these are not the same population (carrier sense range != connectivity range), and state the bias direction. (4) Consider decomposing delivery change into a connectivity term (component reachability, held at alpha=0) and an airtime term (ratio at the swept alpha to the alpha=0 baseline), so the plot literally separates the two effects.

findings.title: Contention is sampled at a single instant per episode (once-per-episode settle), so the airtime model never sees time-varying contention — the spec acknowledges the multi-hop consequence but not the measurement consequence
findings.issue: The spec flags that the engine settles each contact once at entry time and that this may under-represent mobile multi-hop. But the same once-per-episode settle (engine.py _settle uses now=entry, and n_local comes from deg computed on the current dt-step's candidate set) means n_contenders is a single snapshot per contact. The README already lists 'contention sampled once per contact' as an optimistic idealization. For an AIRTIME ceiling measurement this is not a minor caveat: real airtime saturation is a sustained, time-integrated phenomenon (a node in a dense pocket is starved for the whole dwell, and the contender set churns). A single-snapshot contention can both under- and over-state the ceiling depending on where in the contact the snapshot falls, and it interacts with mobility (RWP nodes move through density gradients within one contact). The fidelity gate as written (SI growth + 3-hop chain) does NOT exercise time-varying contention at all, so it can pass while the airtime measurement is still invalid.
findings.fix: Add a third fidelity check specific to airtime: in a STATIC dense cluster (no mobility, so contention is genuinely constant), verify the engine's served-blobs-per-contact and busy-time match a closed-form hand calculation of the budget over the analytic dwell. Then, for the mobile path, either (a) charge the budget over sub-intervals as contention changes (re-sample n_local per dt-step within an open episode and integrate), or (b) explicitly bound the error: run the same RWP scenario at two dt values that straddle the contact timescale and show the airtime curve is dt-stable. Document which of single-snapshot vs time-integrated contention you ship, and the bias direction, in the provenance/caveats table.

findings.title: 'Airtime utilization' is a named headline output but is nowhere defined or measured — budget only returns a served-blob COUNT, with no busy-time vs available-time accounting
findings.issue: Section 1 and the Definition of Done both promise 'airtime-utilization-vs-density (mean + CI)' as a co-headline. But neither the spec nor budget.py defines utilization. budget.blobs_transferable returns an integer count of blobs moved; there is no notion of busy time / total channel time, no offered-vs-served distinction, and t_setup (the handshake floor) is subtracted and then discarded rather than accumulated as overhead airtime. Utilization that is not busy-time/available-time is not utilization — and the lens explicitly asks for utilization vs OFFERED load. Without an offered baseline, a flat utilization curve is uninterpretable (is the channel idle because nothing is offered, or saturated?).
findings.fix: Define utilization precisely in the spec before build: utilization = (sum over contacts of charged airtime) / (sum of available contact-time), where charged airtime = t_setup + (served_blobs * blob_size / eff). Have the budget RETURN this airtime (not just the count) so the engine can accumulate per-contact busy time. Report it alongside OFFERED airtime (what would be needed to move every blob the peer lacks) so utilization is read against demand. Add the setup-floor airtime to the numerator explicitly — at high density with many short contacts, t_setup dominates and is the real airtime sink; a count-only metric hides this.

findings.title: Latency under RWP is survivorship-censored near the knee — mean latency of delivered messages will look FLAT or DECREASE as delivery collapses, which is the opposite of the true effect
findings.issue: metrics.latencies() returns delivery times only for fair-chance messages that were actually delivered. As density passes the saturation knee and delivery ratio falls, the surviving (delivered) population is increasingly the lucky, short-path, low-contention messages; the slow ones time out (TTL) and silently drop out of the latency sample. This is textbook competing-risks / right-censoring (confirmed as a known DTN-evaluation pitfall). The result: reported mean/median latency can stay flat or even improve right where the system is getting worse, badly misleading the airtime-ceiling story. The spec lists latency as headline #2 with zero treatment of censoring. The fair-chance denominator correctly handles end-of-run censoring for DELIVERY, but does nothing for the latency distribution's selection bias.
findings.fix: Treat latency as censored data. (1) Always report latency JOINTLY with delivery ratio on the same density point, and never quote a latency without its delivery context. (2) Report the latency distribution conditioned on a FIXED delivery quantile or use a Kaplan-Meier-style estimator that counts TTL-expired messages as censored at TTL, not as missing. (3) Prefer reporting time-to-X%-delivery (e.g. T50, fraction delivered by t) over mean-of-delivered, since the former is robust to the survivorship bias. State the censoring explicitly in the README caveats.

findings.title: Saturation-knee estimator is unspecified; reusing the 0.5-crossing/midpoint_with_ci machinery is invalid for a non-monotone (rise-then-fall) curve
findings.issue: The Definition of Done requires 'saturation knee reported with a CI,' and Section 4 says 'bootstrap saturation-knee estimate.' But the only crossing estimator in slice-1 (scenario.crossing_0p5 / midpoint_with_ci) finds where a MONOTONE curve crosses 0.5. The airtime headline is explicitly a rise-then-FALL curve. crossing_0p5 will either (a) find the wrong (rising) crossing, or (b) return the first sign change and ignore the peak entirely. A 'knee' is also ambiguous: is it the argmax of delivery (peak), the onset of the binding constraint, or the inflection of the down-slope? Each is a different quantity with a different estimator, and the spec picks none.
findings.fix: Define the knee unambiguously and pick a matching estimator. Recommended: knee = density of peak circulated-blobs/min (argmax), with a bootstrap CI over replications on the argmax (the per-rep matrix already exists in scenario.sweep as per_rep_ratios). Guard against the discrete-grid artifact (argmax pinned to a sweep point) by fitting a smooth (e.g. quadratic-in-log-density) local model around the peak and reporting the fitted argmax + bootstrap CI. Do NOT reuse crossing_0p5. Add a test that a synthetic rise-then-fall curve yields the planted peak within the CI.

findings.title: Binding-constraint diagnostic must be promoted from a reported number to a GATE on the airtime claim
findings.issue: The spec says if the binding fraction is ~0 'we must say so,' but still lists the airtime curves as headline Definition-of-Done items regardless. Given finding #1 (the down-turn can be a formula artifact), a low binding fraction means the headline 'airtime ceiling' is unsupported — you'd be publishing a contention story with no contention. 'Say so' in prose is too weak when the figure caption will say 'airtime saturation.'
findings.fix: Make it a hard gate: the airtime-saturation claim/figure is only published if the binding fraction exceeds a pre-registered threshold (e.g. >X% of contacts at and beyond the knee density) AND the alpha=0 control does NOT turn over at the same density. Otherwise the curve is labelled and reported as connectivity/buffer-limited, not airtime-limited. Pre-register the threshold and the control comparison in the spec.

findings.title: Buffer cap and TTL are uncontrolled confounders that can produce a density turn-down indistinguishable from airtime
findings.issue: buffer.py evicts oldest-by-creation when len > cap; metrics fair-chance uses TTL. Under RWP at high density a node meets many peers, fills its bounded buffer, and evicts — so the target's copy can be evicted before delivery, dropping delivery as density rises. That is a BUFFER effect, not airtime, but it co-varies with density and will contaminate the airtime curve. The spec's airtime-budget sweep controls for the budget but not for buffer_cap or TTL.
findings.fix: Add buffer_cap and TTL as explicit controls. At minimum, run one airtime sweep with effectively-infinite buffer_cap and TTL (as the percolation gate already does) to show whether the turn-down survives — if delivery still falls with cap=inf and TTL=inf, the cause is airtime/contention; if it disappears, it was buffer/TTL. Report this control overlay. Pre-register buffer_cap and TTL values in the provenance table with their bias direction.

findings.title: Linear 1/(1+alpha*n) contention model likely understates dense-crowd collapse; sensitivity check is named but not specified as a validity instrument
findings.issue: BLE discovery/advertising on the 3 shared channels exhibits collision-induced latency that rises super-linearly (effectively exponentially) with device count once contention sets in (ALOHA-style analysis of the 3 advertising channels). The primary model goodput = ideal/(1+alpha*n) is sub-linear-degradation (throughput ~ 1/n), which is OPTIMISTIC relative to a collision regime where success probability itself decays. The spec proposes a 'CSMA-style alternative to sensitivity-test' but does not say how the two will be COMPARED for validity, only that both exist.
findings.fix: Specify the sensitivity check as a measurement: report the knee density and peak delivery under BOTH the 1/(1+alpha*n) model and the CSMA/collision alternative on the same axes, and treat the spread between them as a model-uncertainty band on the headline. State explicitly that the linear model is an upper bound and the collision model the more pessimistic bound, so readers see the ceiling as a range, not a point. Ground both forms' parameters (the 3-channel collision probability) in the cited ALOHA/BLE-collision literature in the provenance table.

findings.title: Density axis: nominal vs empirical degree divergence under RWP is recorded but not used to plot, risking a mislabeled x-axis
findings.issue: scenario.sweep computes density n from the nominal reduced density (density_to_n) and separately records empirical_mean_degree sampled during the run, plus a stationarity_ok flag. Under RWP with walls boundary (or any non-uniform stationary distribution), the realized mean degree can drift from the nominal Poisson value, so plotting against the nominal 'density' mislabels the x-axis and can shift the apparent knee. The spec does not say which axis the headline uses.
findings.fix: Plot the headline curves against EMPIRICAL mean degree (already measured per run), or assert via the existing stationarity_ok gate that empirical == nominal within tolerance and fail the point otherwise. State the chosen axis in the spec. This matters directly for where the knee 'is' and for comparability with the slice-1 d_c=4.51 reference (which is defined against the reduced density).

findings.title: 'Circulated-blobs-per-minute' is undefined and time-normalization is ambiguous across the warmup/measure/drain timeline
findings.issue: Headline #1 and DoD #2 require 'circulated-blobs/min,' but neither spec nor code defines the numerator (engine.transmissions counts accepted offers, including duplicates-to-different-nodes and non-cohort traffic?) or the time denominator (measure_window only? including drain?). engine.transmissions in engine.py increments on every accepted offer across the whole run including warmup-era and drain-era exchanges. A per-minute rate computed over the wrong window will mis-scale the airtime headline and the knee.
findings.fix: Define circulated-blobs/min precisely: numerator = accepted blob transfers during the measure_window only (reset/snapshot the counter at warmup end and at measure-window end), denominator = measure_window in minutes. Decide and document whether duplicate floods count (they are real airtime, so probably yes) and whether non-cohort background traffic is included. Add a test pinning the count over a known mini-scenario.

missing: A precise mathematical definition of 'airtime utilization' (busy-time / available-time, including the t_setup floor) and an OFFERED-airtime baseline to read it against — currently named as a headline but undefined anywhere in spec or code.

An explicit alpha=0 (and cap=inf, TTL=inf) CONTROL overlay on the same axes as every airtime curve, so a density turn-down can be attributed to airtime vs connectivity/buffer/TTL. This is the single most important missing instrument for the lens.

A precise, non-monotone-aware definition of the 'saturation knee' (recommend: argmax of circulated-blobs/min) and a matching bootstrap-on-argmax estimator with a smoothing guard against grid-pinning — NOT the existing 0.5-crossing/midpoint_with_ci, which assumes monotonicity.

Censoring/competing-risks handling for latency under RWP: report latency jointly with delivery, use Kaplan-Meier-style estimation treating TTL expiry as censored, or report time-to-X% delivery instead of mean-of-delivered. The spec lists latency as a headline with no censoring treatment.

Promotion of the binding-constraint fraction from a reported diagnostic to a hard, pre-registered GATE: the airtime-saturation claim is only published if binding fraction > threshold at/beyond the knee AND the alpha=0 control does not also turn over.

An airtime-specific fidelity check (static dense cluster: engine served-blobs and busy-time match a closed-form budget calculation, plus dt-stability of the mobile airtime curve). The two proposed gate checks (SI growth, 3-hop chain) exercise multi-hop but never exercise time-varying contention, which is the core airtime mechanism.

A justified mapping (with bias direction) from the contention proxy n_local = graph degree within radius r to actual co-channel BLE contenders — carrier-sense/interference range differs from connectivity range, and this mapping is what makes the airtime term separable from the density axis.

Specification of which x-axis the headline uses (nominal reduced density vs empirical mean degree) and a stationarity-gated assertion that they agree, so the knee location and the comparison to slice-1's d_c=4.51 are not mislabeled.

A precise definition of circulated-blobs/min (numerator counted over the measure_window only with the counter snapshotted at window boundaries; explicit decision on counting duplicate floods and background traffic) with a pinning test.

Specification of how the 1/(1+alpha*n) model and the CSMA/collision alternative are compared as a model-uncertainty BAND on the headline knee/peak (not just 'both exist'), grounded in the 3-channel ALOHA/BLE-collision literature, since the linear form is optimistic vs the empirically super-linear collision regime.

reviews.lens: ENGINE-FIDELITY: Is the proposed "match the analytic SI/logistic curve" fidelity gate the right one to trust the once-at-entry dynamic engine for a MOBILE multi-hop AIRTIME study?
strengths: Correctly identifies the right blocker. The spec is honest that the dynamic engine is unproven relative to the validated static component-reachability path, and refuses to publish a mobile curve the engine cannot faithfully produce (Section 2, Decision 7). That instinct is exactly right and rare.

The binding-constraint diagnostic (Q3 / DoD line in Section 6) is the single most important guard in the spec: if the fraction of airtime-bound contacts is ~0 across the sweep, the slice is still measuring connectivity not airtime. This converts a possible silent failure into a reported number. Keep it and make it a hard gate, not just an output.

Demanding BLE-grounded provenance for goodput_ideal/t_setup/contact-duration (Section 3) rather than invented numbers is correct; the airtime conclusions are only as defensible as those three constants, and contact duration is exactly the quantity the literature says governs bytes-per-contact.

Reusing the slice-1 determinism contract (disjoint SeedSequence substreams, per-(density,rep,component) independence) and the stationarity acceptance gate on RWP is the right foundation; the engine's per-episode analytic contact timing (dt-independent durations, single t_setup charge across run_until slicing) is already test-covered and genuinely good.

Including a CSMA-style alternative as a sensitivity check on the 1/(1+alpha*n) contention form (Decision 7, Section 3) is the right way to keep the headline from depending on one unverified functional choice.

findings.title: The proposed SI/logistic gate validates the engine in exactly the regime where slice-2's questions do NOT live
findings.issue: The analytic SI/logistic curve I(t)=N/(1+(N-1)e^{-beta t}) is the MEAN-FIELD FLUID LIMIT of epidemic routing. The literature is explicit that this limit (a) abstracts away the spatial dimension, (b) assumes clusters of size two (one infected meets one susceptible), (c) assumes a single copy transfers per meeting, and (d) treats contacts as infinitesimal point-meetings with a Poisson rate (for RWP, lambda=2*omega*r*E[V*]/L^2). Homogeneous mixing provably HOLDS under RWP when range is small vs region. So the engine can match the SI curve PERFECTLY while being wrong about every mechanism this slice exists to measure: finite contact duration, airtime contention (alpha, t_setup, p_fail), multi-copy-per-contact, and spatial correlation at crowd density. To even make the engine reproduce SI you must set alpha~=0, t_setup~=0, p_fail~=0, blob_size tiny, cap=inf, ttl=inf -- i.e. turn OFF the airtime model. A gate that only passes with the feature-under-test disabled is not a fidelity gate for that feature.
findings.fix: Keep the SI/logistic match but DEMOTE it to a 'sanity/plumbing' test (engine reproduces well-mixed exponential spread in the zero-cost limit, AND the empirical pairwise meeting rate matches lambda_RWP=2*omega*r*E[V*]/L^2 within tolerance -- this independently validates the contact-timing layer). Then ADD the gates that actually bound the airtime regime (see findings below): a finite-contact-duration multi-copy test, a multi-hop-across-time test with delivery-time-correct latency, and a contention monotonicity test. The DoD must state that passing SI alone does NOT license the mobile airtime curve.

findings.title: _settle reads buffer state at episode END but stamps the exchange (and thus latency) at episode ENTRY -- a time inconsistency that corrupts the headline latency metric
findings.issue: In engine.py _settle (lines 114-126), the episode is settled when the pair leaves range or at finalize(), so self.buffers[i]/[j] are read with their CURRENT (episode-end / end-of-run) contents -- but now=entry and on_deliver is called with now=entry. Metrics.on_deliver records delivered_at[bid]=now=entry (metrics.py line 31), and latency = delivered_at - created (metrics.py line 48). So a blob that only became available to node i partway through the run, then is handed to j when their long contact finally settles, is recorded as delivered at the contact's ENTRY time -- which can be long before it was even creatable at i. Latency vs density is a co-headline of this slice (Q2). It is being computed against the wrong timestamp and will be systematically UNDER-estimated, and in pathological cases negative (delivered_at < created). No test currently asserts latency sign or ordering.
findings.fix: Decide the episode's exchange time and READ buffers at that same time. Cleanest: settle the contact at ENTER and read buffers at enter (matches the stamp), OR (preferred for fidelity) deliver blobs at the time they become available during the contact and stamp on_deliver with that delivery time. Add a test asserting delivered_at >= created for every delivered blob, and a test that latency increases with hop count. Until fixed, the latency curve must not be published.

findings.title: Single-pass _exchange cannot model repeated exchange as a buffer grows during one long contact (concern b)
findings.issue: _exchange (lines 128-144) does exactly one i->j sweep then one j->i sweep, total <= k blobs, computed from k=blobs_transferable(duration,...). It never re-iterates within the episode. So if node B receives blobs from A early in a long contact and C is also in contact with B for that whole duration, B cannot relay A's blobs onward to C within the same contact window even though airtime and time permit it. At gathering scale RWP produces exactly these long, overlapping, multi-party contacts; this is the 'epidemic burst inside one long contact' the slice is meant to capture. The whole-blob budget k correctly bounds TOTAL bytes, but the single-pass structure additionally and silently bounds HOPS-per-contact to one, which is a separate, unjustified restriction.
findings.fix: Within an episode, iterate exchange rounds (each round consuming from the shared k airtime budget, each round able to pick up blobs that arrived in the prior round) until either k is exhausted or no new blob is transferable -- i.e. an episode-local fixpoint capped by airtime, not a single pass. The static settle_static_fixpoint already shows the iterate-to-fixpoint pattern; the dynamic version must keep the k cap. This is the core engine refinement the spec hand-waves as 'allow repeated exchange as buffers grow' -- specify it as: per-episode, loop offer-rounds while remaining_budget>0 and progress is made.

findings.title: Multi-hop within a single dt step is order-dependent on cand iteration order; the gate's '3+ hop across separate contacts' test does not exercise this hazard
findings.issue: Within _process_step, contacts that complete inside the step are settled inline in cand iteration order; an open contact for pair (i,j) only settles when they part or at finalize. If A-B and B-C are both in range during the same step and both settle in that step, whether A's blob reaches C in that step depends purely on whether (A,B) is iterated before (B,C). Across steps it works only because B's buffer is re-read at the next episode's settle. The spec's proposed multi-hop test ('3+ hop chain delivers across SEPARATE contacts as mobility reconfigures') is the EASY case and will pass; it does not pin down the order-dependence in concurrent/overlapping contacts, which is where real crowds live and where determinism could silently depend on pair-iteration order rather than physics.
findings.fix: Add a deterministic multi-hop test with OVERLAPPING contacts (A-B and B-C simultaneously in range for the same window) and assert the delivered set and timestamps are invariant to neighbor_pairs ordering (e.g. shuffle the candidate pair order with a fixed seed and require identical results). If results depend on order, process simultaneous contacts by a physical tie-break (earliest enter time first) so the engine is order-canonical. Document the chosen tie-break.

findings.title: Blobs that arrive mid-contact (concern a) are handled only by accident of settle-time buffer reads, not by design -- and finalize() collapses all still-open contacts to a single end-of-run settle
findings.issue: Concern (a) 'delivering blobs that arrive mid-contact' is partially covered because _settle re-reads current buffers, so a blob present at settle-time is offered. But because settle fires at episode END, a blob that arrives at node i at t1 and is relevant to a contact that opened at t0<t1 and closes at t2>t1 is treated as if it were available for the whole [t0,t2] (stamped at entry). And finalize() (lines 94-103) settles EVERY still-open contact once at the true end using end-of-run degrees -- so for runs where many contacts are still open at drain end, a large batch of deliveries all get stamped at their various entry times in one pass. This conflates 'when available' with 'when the contact existed'. It is not catastrophic for delivery COUNTS but it is wrong for delivery TIMING and for the airtime accounting of when contention occurred.
findings.fix: Once the episode-local iterate-to-fixpoint (finding 3) and the deliver-at-availability-time stamping (finding 2) are in, this largely resolves. Add a targeted test: blob created at node A at time t_c, A-B contact spanning [t0,t2] with t0<t_c<t2; assert B's delivery timestamp is >= t_c (not t0). Confirm finalize() does not back-date deliveries before blob creation.

findings.title: The contention model is never validated against ITS OWN intended behavior; no gate asserts delivery falls as alpha/n rises
findings.issue: The whole thesis of slice-2 is 'delivery rises then FALLS (contention-limited)'. But the proposed gates (SI match + multi-hop) say nothing about the contention term goodput/(1+alpha*n). blobs_transferable (budget.py) is monotone-decreasing in n_local and in alpha by construction, but n_local is computed as max(deg[i],deg[j]) over the WHOLE candidate set including pairs beyond r (deg uses in_range filter -- ok) at a single position snapshot p0; under mobility n_local can swing within an episode and is sampled once. There is no test that, holding geometry fixed, increasing alpha or local degree strictly reduces blobs moved, and none that the saturation knee actually appears.
findings.fix: Add a contention-monotonicity gate: in a fixed dense configuration, sweep alpha (and separately injected n_contenders) and assert circulated-blobs-per-minute is non-increasing and that a knee exists at the binding/marginal boundary. Pin down whether n_local should be the time-averaged contender count over the episode rather than the entry snapshot; if entry-snapshot, justify it as an upper bound (fewer contenders earlier => more optimistic => consistent with the 'upper bound' framing).

findings.title: Per-episode k is computed from a single n_local snapshot and a single duration, so contention and contact length are decoupled from the within-episode dynamics the refinement introduces
findings.issue: Even after the episode-local fixpoint refinement, k=blobs_transferable(duration,n_local) is one number for the whole episode. That is defensible as a shared-pool upper bound, but it means the 'repeated exchange as buffer grows' loop will be bounded by a k that assumed a fixed contender count and fixed duration. For very long contacts whose contender count changes a lot, this can mislabel the binding fraction.
findings.fix: Document explicitly that k is a per-episode UPPER-BOUND budget (consistent with the slice-wide upper-bound framing) and that within-episode rounds consume from that single pool. Optionally, for long episodes, recompute k per-step-segment. Decide and state which; do not leave it implicit.

missing: A precise, written specification of the refined exchange semantics. The spec says 'deliver blobs at the time they become available' and 'allow repeated exchange as buffers grow' but gives no algorithm. Before build, pin: (1) the exchange occurs at ENTER or is event-driven over the contact; (2) within an episode, offer-rounds iterate to a fixpoint capped by the shared airtime budget k; (3) on_deliver is stamped at the actual availability/delivery time, with the invariant delivered_at >= created enforced.

Concrete numeric tolerances and a baseline for EVERY gate. 'Match the SI curve within tolerance' is unfalsifiable as written. Specify: the analytic curve (I(t)=N/(1+(N-1)e^{-beta t}) with beta from the RWP pairwise meeting rate lambda=2*omega*r*E[V*]/L^2 and beta=(N-1)*lambda or the chosen edge convention), the metric (e.g. max abs deviation in infected fraction over the trajectory, or KS on delivery-time CDF), the tolerance (e.g. <=5% with stated CI from >=R reps), and the seed/config. Without the closed form pinned, the gate cannot be coded test-first.

An independent validation of the CONTACT-TIMING layer separate from the epidemic layer. Assert empirical pairwise meeting rate and mean contact duration match the RWP analytic expressions (lambda_RWP and the chord-length-based mean duration), since contact duration is precisely what determines bytes-per-contact. This decouples 'is the geometry/timing right' from 'is the exchange logic right' so a failure localizes.

The order-independence / determinism statement for CONCURRENT contacts. The slice-1 RNG contract guarantees substream independence, but the engine's handling of simultaneous overlapping contacts within a step is iteration-order dependent. State the tie-break (recommend earliest-enter-first) and add a shuffle-invariance test to the DoD.

An explicit decision on the latency timestamp semantics and a guard test. The latency-vs-density co-headline (Q2) cannot be published until on_deliver stamps a defensible time. Add delivered_at >= created and latency-monotone-in-hops to the DoD's 'new tests for the fidelity gate'.

A pass/fail rule tying the binding-constraint diagnostic to publication. The spec mentions reporting the binding fraction but should make it a GATE: if the binding fraction is ~0 across the entire sweep at the chosen airtime levels, the run is labeled 'connectivity, not airtime' and the airtime headline is withheld -- mirroring how slice-1 withheld the dynamic claim.

A statement of what 'refine the engine' costs and whether the static percolation path remains the source of truth for connectivity. Make explicit that the refined dynamic engine must REPRODUCE the slice-1 static component-reachability delivery in the unbounded/zero-cost/static limit (settle_static_fixpoint already exists as the oracle) -- this is a free, strong regression gate the spec omits and should add: dynamic engine with cap=inf, ttl=inf, alpha=0, t_setup=0, static mobility must equal settle_static_fixpoint delivery.

reviews.lens: AIRTIME-MODEL — defensibility of the BLE contention/airtime model for the rise-then-fall saturation claim
strengths: The spec correctly identifies airtime (not storage) as the existential scale wall and makes 'is airtime even binding?' a first-class diagnostic (the binding-constraint fraction). This is the single most important guardrail: it prevents the slice from re-measuring slice-1 connectivity while claiming to measure airtime. Keep it and gate the headline on it.

Honest framing that every number is an UPPER BOUND, and explicit acknowledgement that the dynamic engine is unproven relative to the validated static path. The fidelity gate (reproduce analytic SI/logistic epidemic growth + a multi-hop-over-time check) is the right instinct and directly targets the engine's known weakness: budget.py/engine.py settle each episode ONCE at entry time (engine.py:114-126, `now = entry`), so a node that gains a blob mid-contact cannot forward it within that same contact, and long contacts get a single exchange rather than repeated reconciliation rounds.

The budget already composes the right ingredients: a t_setup floor that zeroes out short contacts (budget.py:21-23), whole-blob quantization (budget.py:25), and a Bernoulli decode-failure thinning via p_fail (budget.py:32). Half-duplex single shared pool split across both directions (engine.py:128-144) is a reasonable abstraction of BLE's one-radio-at-a-time reality.

Plan to ground goodput_ideal / t_setup / contact-duration in a cited provenance table rather than invented constants, and to sweep the airtime budget at >=3 levels (binding/marginal/non-binding). The sweep is what makes the binding-fraction diagnostic meaningful instead of a single arbitrary operating point.

findings.title: The chosen model 1/(1+alpha*n) is mathematically incapable of the rise-then-fall behavior the slice exists to detect
findings.issue: Question 1 asks whether delivery/circulation 'rises then falls (contention-limited) or plateaus,' and the headline is the saturation knee. But goodput = goodput_ideal/(1+alpha*n_contenders) is a monotone fair-sharing/TDMA-style model: per link it only decreases, and aggregate circulated-blobs cannot fall. Sketch: per-node contact rate grows ~n, blobs moved per contact ~ goodput/(1+alpha*n); aggregate per-node circulation ~ n/(1+alpha*n) -> saturates to 1/alpha as n grows. It asymptotes to a plateau and NEVER turns over. So with the primary model the answer to Q1 is structurally pre-determined to be 'plateau' regardless of the data. The real BLE collapse mechanism is contention/collision in the shared advertising+discovery process, which behaves like ALOHA: aggregate throughput G*e^(-G) peaks (~0.368 at offered load G=1) and then COLLAPSES toward zero as offered load grows. The 1/(1+alpha*n) form has no collision term and no discovery cost that grows super-linearly, so it can model graceful degradation but not the cliff the red-team is worried about.
findings.fix: Make the collision-capable model PRIMARY, not a 'sensitivity check.' Add a discovery/contention success factor with an interior maximum, e.g. multiply effective contacts (or goodput) by an ALOHA-like p_success(n) = exp(-beta*n) (collision on the 3 shared adv channels with no hopping) OR add a discovery-time cost that grows with n so t_setup_eff = t_setup + c*n, which makes usable airtime go negative past a density and forces turnover. Then state the falsifiable prediction up front: 'under the contention model we expect a knee at density ~X; under fair-sharing we expect only a plateau; the binding-fraction tells us which regime BLE is in.' If the team insists on shipping 1/(1+alpha*n) as primary, the spec must explicitly downgrade Q1 to 'plateau-or-degrade' and state that rise-then-fall is OUT OF REACH of the primary model -- otherwise the DoD headline ('saturation knee with CI') is asking for a number the model cannot produce.

findings.title: n_contenders is taken as a single static-degree snapshot at entry time, biasing the airtime number optimistic
findings.issue: engine.py:123 sets n_local = max(deg[i], deg[j]) where deg is the instantaneous neighbor count computed once for the step (engine.py:106-112), and the whole episode's budget is charged at entry (engine.py:114-126). So contention is sampled at ONE instant from the geometric degree, not over the contact, and -- more importantly -- it counts neighbors-in-range, not neighbors actually TRANSMITTING. The number of devices contending for airtime in a real crowd is the set advertising/connecting concurrently, which is a function of duty cycle and offered load, typically >> or << instantaneous radio-disk degree depending on the protocol. Using geometric degree conflates 'how many nodes are near' with 'how much radio energy is competing,' and sampling it once understates the variance and the worst case. README already flags 'single-snapshot contention -> optimistic'; this slice doubles down on it while claiming to measure the airtime wall.
findings.fix: Define n_contenders explicitly as an OFFERED-LOAD quantity, not raw degree: n_contenders = (active transmitters within range over the contact), modeled as degree * duty_cycle or as a time-average over the episode (sample deg at >=k sub-instants of [entry,exit] and average, or integrate). At minimum, average degree over the contact rather than taking entry-instant max, and document a duty-cycle multiplier with provenance. Add a unit test that contention rises when a third node is parked in range for the contact's duration. Report a sensitivity row for n_contenders = degree vs degree*duty_cycle vs a 2-hop interference count, since the conclusion is highly sensitive to this definition.

findings.title: alpha, goodput_ideal, t_setup, and 'typical contact duration' are not yet bound to real BLE figures and several proposed groundings are category errors
findings.issue: The provenance table is named but unspecified, and the natural numbers do not map cleanly onto the model. (1) goodput_ideal: BLE app goodput spans ~100 kbps (1M PHY, 20-byte ATT, no DLE) to ~221 kbps (1M+DLE) to ~1.4 Mbps (2M+DLE) -- a >10x range that dominates every result; picking the high end makes the airtime number wildly optimistic. (2) t_setup: the spec frames it as 'handshake/discovery,' but real BLE discovery latency under contention is 10-50 ms best case, 300 ms-2 s typical on mobile OSes, and can reach tens of seconds at low scan duty cycle -- and it GROWS with density (collisions). A constant t_setup hides exactly the term that produces the cliff. (3) contact duration: human contact durations are power-law (exponent ~ -1.5 to -2.5, seconds to hours), so a single 'typical' mean is meaningless and a mean-based budget mis-handles the mass of very short contacts that t_setup kills. (4) alpha has no physical referent at all in the current form.
findings.fix: Pin the provenance table to: goodput_ideal from a cited measured BLE study (e.g. the ~221.7 kbps practical 1M+DLE figure or Silicon Labs/Punch Through measured numbers), and run the sweep at a CONSERVATIVE goodput (no-DLE ~100 kbps) as the headline with the optimistic value as an upper sensitivity. Make t_setup density-dependent (t_setup(n) grounded in measured discovery-latency-vs-advertiser-count curves) instead of a constant. Derive alpha (or the ALOHA beta) from a cited collision/aggregate-throughput-vs-n curve so it has units and a source, not a tuning knob. For contact duration, do NOT assume a mean: the geometry already produces a real contact-duration distribution from RWP -- report that distribution and confirm it has a power-law-ish tail consistent with the contact-network literature; if it doesn't, flag RWP as optimistic for short contacts. Cite: practical BLE throughput study (IEEE 8614763), Silicon Labs throughput docs, Punch Through throughput series, BLE neighbor-discovery collision analyses (PMC5375785), and human-contact-duration datasets (Cambridge iMote / SocioPatterns).

findings.title: The binding-constraint diagnostic, as specified, can report 'binding' for the wrong reason and won't distinguish the two failure modes
findings.issue: The diagnostic is 'fraction of contacts where the budget bound' (blobs_transferable < blobs the node wanted to send). But in budget.py a contact is 'bound' whenever usable airtime < demand, which happens trivially for SHORT contacts (duration just over t_setup) even at zero contention. So a high binding-fraction could be entirely a t_setup/short-contact artifact, not contention -- exactly the confusion the diagnostic is meant to dispel. It also doesn't separate 'bound by setup floor' from 'bound by contention (alpha*n)' from 'bound by p_fail/quantization.'
findings.fix: Decompose the binding diagnostic into mutually exclusive causes per contact: setup-starved (usable<=0 from t_setup), contention-limited (k limited by the alpha*n term -- i.e. k at n>0 is materially below k at n=0), quantization-limited (gross rounds to 0), and demand-limited (peer wanted fewer than k). Report each fraction across the sweep. The headline 'airtime is binding' should be specifically the contention-limited fraction, not aggregate binding. Add a control: re-run with alpha=0 and confirm the contention-limited fraction goes to 0 (sanity check that the diagnostic isolates the right term).

findings.title: Half-duplex shared pool is charged once per episode, so repeated/incremental exchange in long contacts is impossible -- this biases against the very multi-round reconciliation that real BLE does and that the airtime model should reward or penalize
findings.issue: engine.py:114-144 settles the whole episode at entry with one budget draw and one round of select_offers in each direction. A long contact that in reality would do many connection-event reconciliation rounds (and would let a blob received early be forwarded onward, or let buffers that grow during the contact be re-offered) gets a single shot. The spec's own fidelity-gate section flags this for the SI/multi-hop check, but the AIRTIME consequence is unstated: charging airtime as a lump at entry both (a) ignores that throughput is delivered incrementally over connection intervals and (b) prevents the model from showing that long contacts amortize t_setup -- a key real-world effect that changes the optimistic/pessimistic balance.
findings.fix: When the engine is refined for the fidelity gate, also make the budget incremental: charge airtime per connection-interval (or per dt sub-step) over [entry,exit] using the contention sampled at that sub-step, allowing forward-this-round blobs to participate next round. This makes t_setup a one-time cost amortized over long contacts (correct) and lets contention vary within a contact. Add a test: a single long contact with t_setup>0 transfers strictly more under incremental charging than under lump charging, and a blob received in round 1 can be forwarded to a third node entering mid-episode.

findings.title: p_fail as a flat per-blob Bernoulli mis-models reconciliation decode failure and interacts wrongly with contention
findings.issue: budget.py:32 thins the transferable count by an i.i.d. binomial(gross, 1-p_fail). Real reconciliation (IBLT/set-recon, parent design's mechanism) fails as a SET operation -- a decode failure typically loses the whole reconciliation round (correlated, all-or-nothing) or degrades with the symmetric-difference size, not independently per blob. Worse, p_fail is constant while in reality decode failure rises with contention (collisions corrupt packets), so the model decouples failure from the exact density axis the slice is studying.
findings.fix: Either (a) model p_fail as a per-ROUND failure (with prob p_fail the whole exchange yields 0, matching set-reconciliation semantics) and document it, or (b) make p_fail(n) increase with contention from a cited packet-error-vs-collision curve. State which semantics p_fail has in the provenance table. If keeping per-blob Bernoulli, justify it explicitly as an upper bound (independence is optimistic vs correlated round failure).

missing: A falsifiable, written prediction of WHAT shape the curve should take under each candidate model BEFORE running, with a pass/fail criterion. Right now 'find the knee' is open-ended; given finding #1, the spec must state that the primary model can only plateau and that detecting a true knee REQUIRES the collision model. Without this the slice risks reporting 'no knee found -> airtime is fine,' which would be an artifact of the model, not a result about BLE.

An explicit definition of n_contenders (degree vs active-transmitter offered load vs duty-cycle-scaled), and where in the episode it is sampled (entry instant vs time-average). This is the single most outcome-determining choice in the airtime model and the spec leaves it implicit.

The provenance table's actual rows with chosen values, ranges, and citations, plus a stated headline-vs-sensitivity policy (run conservative goodput as headline, optimistic as upper bound). Naming the table is not grounding it; the >10x goodput range and the 100x t_setup range mean the conclusion is undetermined until these are pinned.

A density-dependent t_setup(n) / discovery-cost term. A constant setup floor removes the dominant real-world contention mechanism (discovery collisions) from the model that is explicitly about contention.

A decomposition of the binding-constraint diagnostic into setup-starved / contention-limited / quantization-limited / demand-limited, with the alpha=0 control. The aggregate 'bound' fraction cannot answer 'is airtime binding' on its own.

A statement of the airtime model's bias DIRECTION for each new assumption (single-snapshot contention -> optimistic; lump-sum-at-entry vs incremental -> sign depends; independent p_fail -> optimistic; no inter-link interference beyond own neighbors -> optimistic), extending the README fidelity table to slice-2's new mechanics so the 'upper bound' label is auditable rather than asserted.

A unit / contention test asserting the model produces an interior maximum (rise-then-fall) under the collision model and only a plateau under fair-sharing -- i.e. a regression test that the two models are actually distinguishable, so the sensitivity check is meaningful rather than two parameterizations of the same monotone curve.

strengths: Correctly front-loads the honest blocker: the spec opens (§2) by naming that slice-1's validated headline used the engine-free static path and the dynamic engine is unproven, and makes the fidelity gate a hard prerequisite (DoD #1) that gates publishing any mobile curve. This is exactly the right intellectual posture given the engine settles each contact once-per-episode at entry time (engine.py _settle, line 114-126).

The binding-constraint diagnostic (§1.3 / DoD #2) is the single most valuable addition: it forces the slice to prove it is measuring airtime and not re-measuring connectivity. Slice-1 infra makes this cheap to compute (AirtimeBudget.blobs_transferable already has all the inputs; just instrument whether the budget bound vs. the missing-blob count bound).

Strong reuse posture (§4): RWP burn-in + stationarity gate, run_one timeline, mean_ci Student-t, the injected-RNG disjoint-substream contract, and density_to_n are all real, shipped, tested primitives (scenario.py, mobility.py, config.py). The spec leans on them rather than reinventing, which is the right call.

Every output is labelled an upper bound and a parameter-provenance table is required (§3, DoD #3, #5), continuing slice-1's discipline (README already has a fidelity/bias table). Sensitivity-testing the contention form (1/(1+alpha*n) vs CSMA-style) instead of betting on one functional form is good scientific hygiene.
The decisions-to-confirm section (§7) surfaces the three real forks (refine-vs-measure-as-is, contention-model form, RWP-vs-clustered mobility) for explicit sign-off rather than burying them.

findings.title: Slice bundles three independently-riskable deliverables; engine-refinement must be split into its own gated task/PR before any measurement
findings.issue: The slice bundles (a) dynamic-engine refinement, (b) a new BLE airtime/contention model, and (c) a new mobile delivery+airtime measurement with a knee estimator. These have different risk profiles and the spec itself admits (a) may require open-ended rework ('refinement is part of this slice', §2). An engine refinement that changes exchange semantics (deliver mid-contact, allow repeated exchange) will silently move the slice-1 percolation numbers and the dynamic-vs-static relationship, yet the measurement code is being built against the same engine in the same PR. If the fidelity gate fails late, the whole measurement PR is blocked, and a reviewer cannot tell whether a surprising curve is an engine bug or a real airtime effect. The DoD treats the gate as a checkbox inside one deliverable rather than a phase boundary.
findings.fix: Restructure into two sequenced tasks/PRs with a hard checkpoint between them. PR-1 (engine fidelity): implement the SI/logistic gate + multi-hop-over-time gate as failing tests FIRST (TDD), refine the engine until green, and prove the refinement does NOT regress the shipped percolation gate (test_integration_percolation.py oracle-KAT and susceptibility-peak must still pass bit-for-bit or with an explicitly-justified delta). PR-1 ships and is reviewed/merged on its own. PR-2 (airtime model + measurement) builds only on the now-trusted engine. Update §6 loop gates to make 'engine fidelity gate passes AND percolation gate unregressed' a merge gate for PR-1, separate from the measurement sign-off.

findings.title: Fidelity gate is under-specified: no tolerance, no analytic reference parameters, no determinism/seed-count spec, no regression guard on the percolation gate
findings.issue: DoD #1 says the gate is 'SI/epidemic growth match + multi-hop-over-time' but gives no pass criterion that an implementer or reviewer can apply. Missing: (1) the analytic reference — for a dense well-mixed population epidemic/SI infection follows the logistic I(t)=I0*K/(I0+(K-I0)e^{-beta*K*t}); the spec must state which closed form, how beta is derived from contact rate (beta from mean pairwise contact rate = mean_degree * something / N, which the sim can measure independently), and the population/density regime where mean-field SI is even valid (it is NOT valid near d_c where the graph is fragmented — the gate must run in a clearly supercritical, well-mixed regime or it will 'fail' for correct reasons). (2) A numeric tolerance band and the statistic (max abs deviation of the I(t) curve? time-to-50%? both with a CI over seeds?). (3) Seed count / determinism: the gate is itself a stochastic measurement and needs the same per-rep CI treatment as the headline. (4) No requirement that the refined engine leave the slice-1 percolation gate intact — this is the biggest silent-regression risk.
findings.fix: Specify in §2/§6: the exact analytic SI logistic form and how its rate constant is fit/derived from independently-measured contact statistics; the validity regime (e.g. d>=8, torus, RWP well-mixed, burn-in'd); the pass statistic + tolerance (e.g. 'measured I(t) within +/-X% of analytic across the growth phase, over >=Nseeds, reported with CI') with X justified, not invented; the multi-hop check made concrete (a deterministic 3-node relay where A,B,C are never simultaneously in range but B carries A's blob to C across two disjoint contacts — assert delivery, assert it FAILS if the engine only settled at the first contact's entry); and an explicit regression assertion that the refined engine reproduces test_integration_percolation.py results.

findings.title: Contention model form is likely mis-grounded: BLE advertising aggregate throughput behaves like ALOHA (multiplicative collision decay), not 1/(1+alpha*n)
findings.issue: The spec ships goodput = goodput_ideal/(1+alpha*n) as PRIMARY with CSMA as the sensitivity check (§3, §7). But the literature models BLE advertising aggregate throughput as an ALOHA network (three advertising channels with no real collision avoidance), whose success probability decays like e^{-2*G} / e^{-k*n}, i.e. throughput rises then COLLAPSES with offered load — a fundamentally different (and harsher) shape than the monotone-asymptotic 1/(1+alpha*n), which never collapses. Since the headline question (§1.1) is explicitly 'does delivery rise then FALL (contention-limited)?', the choice of contention form largely DETERMINES the answer: 1/(1+alpha*n) makes a true rise-then-fall in airtime-per-contact impossible (per-contact goodput is monotone), so any knee would come only from mobility/degree effects, not contention collapse. CSMA-style is also not the natural alternative here — connectionless BLE advertising is ALOHA-like, not CSMA (CSMA implies carrier sensing that BLE advertising does not do). Picking the gentler model as 'primary' biases toward 'airtime is not binding'.
findings.fix: Reframe §3: treat the functional form as a first-class sensitivity axis, not a primary+afterthought. Include at least one model that can actually collapse (ALOHA-style e^{-beta*n} or an offered-load curve), grounded in the cited BLE-advertising-as-ALOHA result, alongside 1/(1+alpha*n). State explicitly that the rise-then-fall finding is conditional on the contention form, and report the knee under each. Drop or re-justify 'CSMA' — for connectionless advertising the defensible alternative is ALOHA, not CSMA. Add the provenance citation (BLE advertising aggregate throughput ~ ALOHA) to the README table.

findings.title: n_contenders semantics are ambiguous and the current implementation conflates 'pair degree' with 'channel contenders'
findings.issue: §3 defines n_contenders = 'peers within range during the contact', but the engine currently uses n_local = max(deg[i], deg[j]) (engine.py line 123) — the larger one-hop degree of the two endpoints. For an airtime/contention model the physically relevant quantity is the number of radios contending for the SAME channel airtime in the SAME neighborhood, which is closer to the union of i and j's neighbors (a local clique size), and it is also time-varying within a contact (the spec says 'during the contact' but the engine samples degree once per step from p0). Using max-degree under-counts dense overlaps and is sampled at a single instant. This directly feeds the binding diagnostic and the knee, so the ambiguity is load-bearing.
findings.fix: Define n_contenders precisely in §3 (recommend: size of the combined neighbor set of i and j within range, or a stated approximation) and state how/when it is sampled within an episode. If kept as an entry-time snapshot, label that as an additional optimism (fewer contenders than a busy contact would see) in the bias table. Add a unit test pinning the contender count for a hand-placed cluster.

findings.title: Saturation-knee estimator is named but not defined; reusing the delivery=0.5 crossing machinery is wrong for a non-monotone curve
findings.issue: DoD #2/#3 require a 'saturation knee reported with a CI' and §4 says 'bootstrap saturation-knee estimate', but no estimator is defined. The existing slice-1 bootstrap machinery (scenario.py crossing_0p5 / midpoint_with_ci) finds a 0.5 LEVEL crossing of a monotone curve — it is the wrong tool for a knee/peak of a rise-then-fall curve. crossing_0p5 returns NaN when there is no 0.5 crossing and finds the first sign change, not a maximum or an elbow. A knee on a non-monotone or saturating curve needs a different definition (argmax of circulated-blobs/min? the elbow via max-curvature or a piecewise-linear breakpoint? the density where binding-fraction first exceeds 0.5?). Without a definition the bootstrap CI is meaningless and the result is not reproducible.
findings.fix: Define the knee estimator explicitly in §4 (pick one: argmax of throughput, or Kneedle/max-curvature elbow, or piecewise-linear breakpoint), state how it degrades when the curve is monotone (report 'no knee in range' rather than NaN), and bootstrap THAT statistic over replications (reuse the per-rep matrix + percentile pattern from midpoint_with_ci but with the new point estimator). Add a test on a synthetic rise-then-fall curve with a known knee.

findings.title: Latency is a headline output (§1.2) but slice-1 has no fair-chance/censoring treatment for latency, only for delivery ratio
findings.issue: §1.2 makes latency vs density a co-headline and DoD #2 implies latency reporting. But metrics.latencies() (metrics.py line 47) returns delivered-only latencies over the fair-chance set, with no handling of the heavy right-censoring that DTN carry-and-forward produces: under RWP at low density most messages are delivered slowly or not at all, so the mean of delivered-only latencies is severely optimistically biased (it conditions on success, discarding the slow/never tail). Reporting mean delivered latency vs density without stating this will systematically understate latency exactly where the slice's thesis (slow DTN delivery) is most important. The spec inherits this metric without flagging it.
findings.fix: Specify the latency statistic and its censoring treatment in §1.2/§4: report a distribution (median + high percentiles) not just a mean; state explicitly that latencies are conditional-on-delivery and therefore a LOWER bound on real latency; consider reporting delivery-ratio-within-T alongside, which is censoring-robust. Add this as an optimism row in the bias table.

findings.title: Engine half-duplex single-pool budget plus mid-contact delivery refinement interact; the refinement's effect on the shared-pool invariant is unspecified
findings.issue: The shared single-pool half-duplex budget is a tested invariant (test_engine.py test_per_episode_shared_budget_is_one_pool_not_per_direction). The proposed refinement 'allow repeated exchange as buffers grow' / 'deliver at the time they become available' changes a long contact from one settle into multiple sub-settlements, which must NOT silently turn the one shared pool into multiple pools (re-charging t_setup or re-granting budget per sub-exchange would inflate goodput and bias delivery up). The spec does not say how the per-episode budget (t_setup charged once, single goodput pool over the analytic duration) is preserved under repeated intra-contact exchange.
findings.fix: State the invariant the refinement must preserve: t_setup charged once per physical episode; the goodput pool is the episode duration's airtime, drawn down by cumulative bytes moved across sub-exchanges, not reset per sub-exchange. Add/extend a test asserting total blobs moved over a long contact with mid-contact arrivals equals the single-pool budget (not N times it).

findings.title: Out-of-scope / deferred list is mostly right but omits two items that the new measurement implicitly depends on
findings.issue: §5 'Out' correctly defers crypto, anonymity, real PHY, bridges, mobile platform. But two things the airtime story leans on are neither in-scope-defined nor explicitly deferred: (1) reconciliation overhead — slice-1 models reconciliation as a pure byte budget with NO IBLT/set-reconciliation protocol overhead (README flags this as optimistic); an airtime slice whose headline is airtime cost should at least name this as a known optimism affecting the very quantity being measured, or scope a sensitivity multiplier. (2) Clustered/'gathering' mobility — §7 defers it to a fast-follow, but the slice's framing ('crowd density', 'gathering scale') is precisely where RWP (open-field uniform) is least representative; the spec should state that the knee under RWP is itself an optimistic bound vs a clustered venue.
findings.fix: Add to §5: explicitly defer set-reconciliation protocol overhead (with a one-line note that measured airtime cost is therefore a lower bound on real airtime / upper bound on delivery), and explicitly state RWP-vs-clustered as a named optimism for the headline (cross-referencing the §7 fast-follow). Both are one-line additions that close honesty gaps in the airtime-cost claim.

missing: A concrete, sequenced two-phase plan (engine-fidelity PR, then measurement PR) with the engine PR gated on BOTH the new fidelity gate AND non-regression of the existing slice-1 percolation gate (oracle-KAT + susceptibility peak).

The exact analytic reference for the fidelity gate: which SI/logistic closed form, how its rate constant is derived from independently-measured contact statistics, the density/mixing regime where mean-field SI is valid (clearly supercritical, not near d_c), the pass statistic, and a justified numeric tolerance with a CI over seeds.

A precise definition of the saturation-knee estimator (argmax / elbow / breakpoint), its behavior when the curve is monotone, and the bootstrap procedure for ITS CI (the existing crossing_0p5/midpoint machinery is for a 0.5-level monotone crossing and does not apply).
A precise definition of n_contenders (combined-neighborhood vs max-degree) and when it is sampled within an episode, plus a pinning unit test.

At least one contention model that can actually collapse (ALOHA/offered-load, grounded in the BLE-advertising-as-ALOHA literature) treated co-equally with 1/(1+alpha*n); a statement that the rise-then-fall headline is conditional on the contention form; and replacement/justification of 'CSMA' (connectionless advertising is ALOHA-like, not CSMA).

The invariant the engine refinement must preserve under repeated intra-contact exchange (t_setup once per physical episode; one shared draw-down pool, not re-granted per sub-exchange) plus a guarding test.

A defined latency statistic with explicit conditional-on-delivery / right-censoring treatment (distribution + percentiles, labelled a lower bound), and ideally a censoring-robust delivery-within-T metric.

A fidelity/bias table update for the new mechanics (contention-form choice, entry-time contender snapshot, RWP-vs-clustered, omitted reconciliation overhead), each tagged with bias direction, matching slice-1's existing table discipline.

mustFix.title: Primary contention model 1/(1+α·n) is mathematically incapable of the rise-then-fall it is built to detect — and its n_local==graph-degree term hard-codes the down-slope it claims to discover
mustFix.fix: Confirmed in code: budget.py:24 sets eff = throughput_ideal/(1+α·n_local) (strictly monotone in n; aggregate circulation n/(1+α·n) asymptotes to a plateau and cannot turn over), and engine.py:123 sets n_local = max(deg[i],deg[j]) where deg is the unit-disk graph degree = density. So either (a) the curve only plateaus (Q1 'rise-then-fall?' is structurally pre-answered 'plateau'), or (b) any down-turn that appears is a formula artifact of dividing throughput by a quantity proportional to the density axis, not emergent contention. SPEC FIX before build: (1) Make a collision-capable model that CAN have an interior maximum PRIMARY (ALOHA-style p_success(n)=exp(-β·n) on the 3 shared advertising channels, or density-dependent t_setup(n)=t_setup+c·n driving usable airtime negative). Connectionless BLE advertising is ALOHA-like, NOT CSMA — drop/rejustify the 'CSMA alternative' framing. (2) Treat the functional form as a first-class sensitivity axis and report knee+peak under each form as a model-uncertainty BAND, stating the linear form is the optimistic bound. (3) State the falsifiable prediction up front (linear=>plateau, collision=>knee at ~X) and add a regression test that the two models are actually distinguishable (interior max vs plateau). (4) Add an α=0 control overlay on the SAME axes for every airtime curve: if α=0 already turns over, the turnover is connectivity/buffer/TTL, not airtime. (5) Justify the n_contenders->co-channel-contenders mapping with bias direction (carrier-sense/interference range != connectivity range).

mustFix.title: Split engine-refinement into its own gated PR before any measurement; pin the fidelity gate's analytic reference, tolerance, validity regime, and add a non-regression guard on the slice-1 percolation gate
mustFix.fix: The slice bundles three independently-riskable deliverables (engine refinement, new airtime model, new measurement) and §2 admits refinement 'may require open-ended rework.' Refining exchange semantics will silently move slice-1 percolation numbers, and a reviewer cannot tell an engine bug from a real airtime effect if both ship in one PR. SPEC FIX: Restructure §6 into two sequenced PRs with a hard checkpoint. PR-1 (engine fidelity): write the SI/logistic + multi-hop gates as FAILING tests first, refine until green, and REQUIRE the refined engine reproduce settle_static_fixpoint (engine.py:150) delivery in the cap=inf/ttl=inf/α=0/t_setup=0/static limit AND leave test_integration_percolation.py (oracle-KAT + susceptibility peak) unregressed bit-for-bit or with an explicitly justified delta — make this a merge gate. PR-2 (airtime model + measurement) builds only on the trusted engine. Pin the gate so it is falsifiable: the exact SI logistic closed form I(t)=N/(1+(N-1)e^{-βt}); how β is DERIVED from the independently-measured RWP pairwise meeting rate λ=2·ω·r·E[V*]/L^2 (and validate empirical meeting rate + mean contact duration match the RWP analytic expressions, decoupling contact-timing fidelity from exchange-logic fidelity); the validity regime (clearly supercritical/well-mixed, NOT near d_c where mean-field SI is invalid); the pass statistic + a justified numeric tolerance with a CI over >=N seeds.

mustFix.title: Specify the refined exchange semantics as an algorithm, fix the latency-timestamp corruption, and preserve the single-pool budget invariant under repeated intra-contact exchange
mustFix.fix: Confirmed: engine.py:118-126 settles at episode END but uses now=entry; metrics.py:25-31 stamps delivered_at=entry and metrics.py:48 computes latency=delivered_at-created, so a blob created mid-contact can be stamped BEFORE its creation (delivered_at < created, negative latency) and latency vs density (co-headline Q2) is systematically under-estimated. _exchange (engine.py:128-144) does a single i->j then j->i pass, silently capping HOPS-per-contact to one. SPEC FIX before build, pin the algorithm: (1) within an episode, iterate offer-rounds to a fixpoint, each round consuming from the SHARED per-episode airtime pool k (NOT re-granted, t_setup charged ONCE per physical episode) and able to pick up blobs that arrived in the prior round; add a test that total blobs over a long contact with mid-contact arrivals equals the single-pool budget, not N times it. (2) Stamp on_deliver at the actual availability/delivery time, enforce delivered_at >= created with a test, plus a latency-monotone-in-hops test. (3) Until both are fixed, the latency curve must not be published. (4) Add a shuffle-invariance test for OVERLAPPING concurrent contacts (A-B and B-C simultaneously in range): assert delivered set + timestamps invariant to neighbor_pairs ordering; if not, canonicalize by earliest-enter-first tie-break and document it.

mustFix.title: Define the saturation-knee estimator for a non-monotone curve — do NOT reuse the 0.5-crossing machinery — and promote the binding-constraint fraction from a reported number to a hard pre-registered GATE
mustFix.fix: Confirmed: the only crossing estimator (scenario.py:140 crossing_0p5 / :149 midpoint_with_ci) finds a MONOTONE 0.5-LEVEL crossing and returns NaN when there is none — wrong tool for a rise-then-fall knee. §1.3 says a ~0 binding fraction means 'we must say so' but the DoD still lists the airtime curves regardless. SPEC FIX: (1) Define the knee unambiguously (recommend argmax of circulated-blobs/min) with a smoothing guard against grid-pinning (fit a local quadratic-in-log-density around the peak), bootstrap THAT statistic over the per_rep matrix already in scenario.sweep (scenario.py:110), state behavior when the curve is monotone ('no knee in range', not NaN), and add a synthetic rise-then-fall test with a planted peak. (2) Make the airtime claim a hard gate: the 'airtime saturation' figure/caption is only published if the CONTENTION-LIMITED binding fraction (decomposed from setup-starved/quantization-limited/demand-limited per contact, with an α=0 control confirming it goes to 0) exceeds a pre-registered threshold at/beyond the knee AND the α=0 control does not also turn over; otherwise label and report the curve as connectivity/buffer/TTL-limited.

mustFix.title: Define 'airtime utilization' and 'circulated-blobs/min' precisely (they are named headlines but undefined), with an offered-load baseline and measure-window normalization
mustFix.fix: Confirmed: §1/DoD#2 promise 'airtime-utilization-vs-density' and 'circulated-blobs/min' but budget.blobs_transferable (budget.py:20) returns only an integer COUNT — no busy-time/available-time, no offered-vs-served, and t_setup is subtracted then discarded rather than accumulated as overhead airtime. engine.transmissions (engine.py:33) counts every accepted offer across warmup+measure+drain. SPEC FIX: (1) utilization = (sum of charged airtime, where charged = t_setup + served_blobs·blob_size/eff) / (sum of available contact-time); have budget RETURN this airtime, not just the count, and report it against OFFERED airtime (what would move every blob the peer lacks) so a flat curve is interpretable. Add t_setup to the numerator explicitly (at high density many short contacts make t_setup the real airtime sink). (2) circulated-blobs/min: numerator = accepted transfers during the measure_window ONLY (snapshot the counter at warmup-end and measure-window-end), denominator = measure_window in minutes; decide+document whether duplicate floods and non-cohort traffic count; add a pinning test on a mini-scenario.

mustFix.title: Treat latency as right-censored / survivorship-biased data; control buffer_cap and TTL as confounders; ground every airtime constant with provenance and bias direction
mustFix.fix: Confirmed: metrics.latencies (metrics.py:47) returns delivered-only fair-chance latencies with no censoring treatment — as delivery collapses past the knee the surviving sample is the lucky short-path messages, so mean latency looks FLAT or IMPROVES exactly where the system worsens. Buffer eviction + TTL co-vary with density (a buffer/TTL turn-down is indistinguishable from airtime). The provenance table is named (§3) but unspecified, and the natural BLE numbers span >10x (goodput_ideal 100kbps no-DLE to 1.4Mbps 2M+DLE) and ~100x (t_setup 10ms to seconds, and it GROWS with density). SPEC FIX: (1) Always report latency JOINTLY with delivery ratio; use a Kaplan-Meier-style estimator counting TTL-expired as censored at TTL, or report time-to-X%-delivery (T50) instead of mean-of-delivered; label delivered-only latency a LOWER bound. (2) Run at least one airtime sweep with cap=inf AND ttl=inf to show whether the turn-down survives (survives => airtime; vanishes => buffer/TTL); pre-register cap and TTL with bias direction. (3) Fill the provenance table with cited values: run a CONSERVATIVE goodput (~100kbps no-DLE) as headline with the optimistic value as upper sensitivity; make t_setup density-dependent from measured discovery-latency-vs-advertiser-count; derive α/β from a cited collision curve; report the RWP-generated contact-duration DISTRIBUTION (not a single mean) and flag if its tail is optimistic vs human-contact data. Extend the README bias table with a row+direction for each new mechanic (single-snapshot/max-degree contention=optimistic; lump-at-entry vs incremental; independent per-blob p_fail=optimistic vs correlated round failure; omitted set-reconciliation overhead=optimistic; RWP-vs-clustered=optimistic for the headline).

shouldConsider: Decide which x-axis the headline uses — nominal reduced density vs empirical mean degree (scenario.sweep already records empirical_mean_degree and a stationarity_ok flag, scenario.py:86-87) — and gate each point on empirical==nominal within tolerance, so the knee location and the comparison to slice-1's d_c=4.51 are not mislabeled.

Sharpen n_contenders semantics: it is currently max(deg[i],deg[j]) sampled once from p0 (engine.py:123); consider the combined neighbor set of i and j (local clique), a time-average over the episode (or a duty-cycle multiplier on degree), with a pinning unit test for a hand-placed cluster. If kept as entry-snapshot, label it explicitly as an additional optimism.

Add an airtime-specific fidelity check beyond SI+multi-hop: a STATIC dense cluster (constant contention) where served-blobs and busy-time match a closed-form hand calculation, plus dt-stability of the mobile airtime curve at two dt straddling the contact timescale — the proposed gates never exercise time-varying contention, which is the core airtime mechanism.

Reconsider p_fail semantics (budget.py:32): per-blob i.i.d. Bernoulli mis-models set-reconciliation, which fails all-or-nothing per round; consider per-round failure or p_fail(n) rising with contention, and document which (and that independence is optimistic).

Add to §5 'Out' an explicit deferral of set-reconciliation protocol overhead (one line: measured airtime is therefore a lower bound on real airtime) and an explicit note that the RWP knee is itself an optimistic bound vs a clustered/gathering venue (cross-ref the §7 fast-follow).

For long contacts where contender count changes a lot, document that k is a per-episode UPPER-BOUND budget computed from a single n_local snapshot and a single duration; optionally recompute k per step-segment and state the choice rather than leaving it implicit.
synthesis.scopeCheck: All four reviewers independently land on needs-rework with strong cross-lens convergence on the same defects, which I verified directly against the code (budget.py:24, engine.py:118-126/123, metrics.py:25-31/47-48, scenario.py:140-165). Scope verdict: YES, engine-refinement must be split out. The spec itself admits (§2) refinement 'may require open-ended rework,' yet §6 treats the fidelity gate as a checkbox inside a single deliverable that also contains the new airtime model and the measurement/knee code. That bundling is the central scope problem: a refinement that changes exchange semantics will silently move slice-1's validated percolation numbers, and a reviewer cannot distinguish an engine bug from a real airtime effect if both ship together. Sequence as PR-1 (engine fidelity, gated on BOTH the new SI/multi-hop gate AND non-regression of the existing percolation oracle-KAT/susceptibility-peak, plus reproducing settle_static_fixpoint in the zero-cost static limit) then PR-2 (airtime model + measurement) on the trusted engine. Two scope items are also under-deferred and should be named in §5: set-reconciliation protocol overhead (the very airtime quantity being measured is modeled with zero overhead) and RWP-vs-clustered mobility (the headline is a 'crowd/gathering' question where open-field RWP is least representative). The remaining in/out boundaries (crypto, anonymity, real PHY, bridges, mobile platform deferred) are correct.
synthesis.signoffRecommendation: DO NOT SIGN OFF to build as written; return to spec revision. The intellectual posture is excellent and rare (honest about the unproven engine; the binding-constraint diagnostic and the upper-bound framing are exactly right), but the spec as written would produce an UNINTERPRETABLE headline: the primary contention model 1/(1+α·n) cannot produce the rise-then-fall it exists to detect, and its n_local==graph-degree term either forces a plateau or bakes a density artifact into any down-turn — so the Definition-of-Done 'saturation knee with CI' asks for a number the model and estimator cannot validly produce. Require the six MUST-FIX items resolved IN THE SPEC before any code: (1) collision-capable primary model + α=0 control, (2) split into an engine-fidelity PR with a hard non-regression gate + pinned analytic reference, (3) specified refined-exchange algorithm with the delivered_at>=created fix, (4) a real non-monotone knee estimator + binding-fraction promoted to a hard gate, (5) precise definitions of utilization and circulated-blobs/min with an offered baseline, (6) censoring-aware latency + cap/TTL controls + a filled provenance table with bias directions. None require new infrastructure — all reuse shipped slice-1 primitives. This is ready-after-fixes in spirit: a focused revision pass plus the PR split, then a TARGETED re-review confirming the six items are addressed (a full fan-out re-run is not needed) before granting build sign-off.
